coaching.coaching: Plan how gaps in acceptance criteria verification coverage will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: gaps in acceptance criteria verification coverage

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean

verify command `grep -q 'make worktree' docs/local-dev-env.md && echo DOC-OK` greps the unanchored word 'make' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_make'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"make":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit shell mechanism for venv activation inside the Makefile recipe (e.g., `$(MAKE) -C $(dir) install` with `PYTHON=$(dir)/.venv/bin/python`, or `bash -c 'source $(dir)/.venv/bin/activate && $(MAKE) -C $(dir) install'`, or `.ONESHELL:`) so an executor does not need to invent it.
evidence: 'then, with the venv active, $(MAKE) install' — the mechanism for achieving activation across subshells is unspecified
Makefiles spawn a new shell per recipe line; `source` does not persist across lines without explicit `bash -c` or `.ONESHELL`
findings.finding: The plan says the venv is activated 'with the venv active' before running `$(MAKE) install`, but does not specify the shell mechanism for achieving this inside a Makefile recipe. Each Makefile recipe line runs in its own subshell, so a bare `source .venv/bin/activate` on one line has no effect on the next line. An executing agent must choose between `bash -c 'source .venv/bin/activate && $(MAKE) install'`, overriding `PATH`, or passing `PYTHON=.venv/bin/python` to the sub-make — none of which is stated. This is a defaultable gap that blocks a literal implementation.
findings.location: What section — 'Provisions the venv via the SAME sequence...' bullet
scenarios: An agent emits a recipe with `source .venv/bin/activate` on one line and `$(MAKE) install` on the next — the venv is not active when make install runs, causing it to install into the wrong Python.
An agent uses `.ONESHELL:` which may change other target behavior in the Makefile.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' bullet: 'Activate the venv by passing the venv Python explicitly: run `cd <dir> && bash -c "source .venv/bin/activate && $(MAKE) install"`; alternatively, declare `.ONESHELL:` in the new target block if the repo's Makefile style permits it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the AC3 `.PHONY` grep to escape the literal dot: `grep -q '^\.PHONY:.*worktree' Makefile` (or use `grep -Fq '.PHONY:' Makefile && grep -q 'worktree' Makefile`).
evidence: Proving command: `grep -q '^.PHONY:.*worktree' Makefile`
In BRE/ERE, `.` inside a bracket expression or standalone matches any character; `^.PHONY:` matches any single character followed by `PHONY:`, not necessarily a literal dot.
findings.finding: The AC3 proving command `grep -q '^.PHONY:.*worktree' Makefile` uses an unescaped `.` as a regex metacharacter, matching any character before `PHONY:` (e.g., `XPHONY:`) instead of the literal `.PHONY:` prefix. This is VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY #1 (unanchored/unescaped pattern). While the practical false-positive risk in a Makefile is low, the command as written is technically incorrect and could silently pass on a malformed file.
scenarios: A Makefile that accidentally has a line like `_PHONY: worktree` would pass the grep, falsely satisfying the criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -q '^.PHONY:.*worktree' Makefile` with `grep -q '^\.PHONY:.*worktree' Makefile` to anchor on a literal dot.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (with a proving command) that verifies `make worktree name=<tmp> dir=<custom-path>` creates the worktree at the specified custom path rather than the default `../<name>`.
evidence: 'Creates the worktree at ../<name> (overridable via dir=<path>)'
No AC in the five listed criteria exercises or verifies the dir= parameter
findings.finding: The What section describes `dir=<path>` as an overridable parameter ('Creates the worktree at ../<name> (overridable via dir=<path>)'), but no acceptance criterion verifies that this override works. This is a described deliverable with no covering criterion.
findings.location: What section (dir=<path> override); Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers dir= override)
scenarios: An implementer omits the dir= wiring entirely and all ACs still pass; the feature is silently broken for users who need a non-default path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '[ ] `make worktree name=__wt_probe dir=/tmp/__wt_probe_custom` creates a worktree at `/tmp/__wt_probe_custom` containing a populated .venv. Proving command: `make worktree name=__wt_probe dir=/tmp/__wt_probe_custom && test -x /tmp/__wt_probe_custom/.venv/bin/rebar && echo DIR-OVERRIDE-OK`; Cleanup: `git worktree remove --force /tmp/__wt_probe_custom && git branch -D __wt_probe`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `trap`-based (or shell `||`) cleanup in the Makefile recipe so that a failure in `python3 -m venv` or `$(MAKE) install` automatically removes the partially-created worktree and branch (e.g., `git worktree remove --force <dir> && git branch -D <name>`), or explicitly acknowledge in the plan that partial-failure cleanup is manual and add recovery instructions to `docs/local-dev-env.md`.
evidence: The plan describes a multi-step recipe (fetch → worktree add → venv → make install) with no error-handling between steps.
AC cleanup command ('git worktree remove --force ../__wt_probe && git branch -D __wt_probe') is only shown for the test probe, not wired into the recipe itself on failure.

T4 checklist: 'Any destructive/irreversible step is an explicit, justified choice' and 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan' — branch+worktree creation is destructive and no rollback is specified for mid-recipe failure.
findings.finding: The Makefile recipe has no rollback/cleanup path for partial failure: if `git worktree add` succeeds but the subsequent `python3 -m venv .venv` or `$(MAKE) install` step fails, the developer is left with a dangling worktree directory and an orphaned local branch. The plan neither specifies a `trap`-style cleanup in the recipe nor acknowledges this partial-failure state as an accepted risk with a manual recovery note.
findings.location: ## What — 'Provisions the venv via the SAME sequence…' / Makefile recipe (unwritten)
scenarios: Network failure during `make install` (pip can't reach PyPI) leaves a worktree at `../<name>` and branch `<name>` that the developer must manually remove before retrying.
Python version mismatch causes `python3 -m venv` to error after the worktree is already created, leaving a half-provisioned directory.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add partial-state cleanup to the `worktree` recipe: on failure (via a `trap` or ordered teardown step), remove the half-created worktree (`git worktree remove --force`) and branch so the command is safe to re-run with the same name.
evidence: Plan specifies sequential shell steps with no `||` cleanup or trap: 'git fetch origin then git worktree add ... then python3 -m venv .venv then $(MAKE) install'
If venv or install fails, the worktree and branch already exist; re-running `make worktree name=<same>` will fail immediately at `git worktree add` with 'already exists'
AC cleanup is only in the test probe, not in the target itself
Absence of any rollback, trap, or `--force` re-use logic in the plan
findings.finding: The multi-step recipe (fetch → worktree add → venv → make install) has no partial-state cleanup on intermediate failure, leaving a dangling worktree+branch that prevents idempotent re-runs with the same name.
findings.location: ## What — recipe steps; ## Acceptance Criteria AC #2
scenarios: Developer runs `make worktree name=my-feature`; network is slow and `make install` fails; they fix their network and re-run — git worktree add fails because branch/dir already exist, leaving them stuck.
CI probe fails mid-run; retry of the same job fails at worktree creation.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Makefile recipe, use a shell trap or chain the cleanup: wrap the body in a subshell with `trap 'git worktree remove --force $(dir) 2>/dev/null; git branch -D $(name) 2>/dev/null' ERR` so any step failure rolls back the worktree and branch before exiting non-zero.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a `make worktree-remove name=<branch>` target (or a `make worktree name=<branch> remove=1` variant) that wraps `git worktree remove --force` + `git branch -D`, or explicitly document in the Scope why teardown is a non-goal and where users should look for cleanup instructions.
evidence: Scope section lists only creation targets and explicitly excludes no-goal on teardown
AC #2 cleanup is documented only as a test artifact command, not as a plan deliverable
Plan's stated motivation: 'agents and humans stop hand-running the multi-command worktree setup' — teardown is equally multi-command (`git worktree remove --force <dir> && git branch -D <name>`)
Absence of a `make worktree-remove name=<branch>` or equivalent in Scope
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retirement/teardown target for the worktrees it creates, leaving lifecycle cleanup as an undocumented manual multi-command sequence — inconsistent with the plan's stated goal of replacing manual multi-command sequences.
scenarios: An agent or developer finishes with a worktree and must look up the two-command manual teardown sequence.
Stale worktrees accumulate in `.git/worktrees/` when users forget the exact cleanup incantation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the remote and base branch configurable via Makefile variables (e.g., `remote=origin` and `base=main` with sensible defaults) so the target is not brittle to repo topology changes.
evidence: Plan states: 'git fetch origin then git worktree add <dir> -b <name> origin/main' with no override variable
No `base=` or `remote=` parameter is mentioned alongside `name=` and `dir=`
The `dir=` override is documented but no equivalent for the base ref
findings.finding: `origin/main` is hardcoded as the base ref with no configuration point; if a repo uses a different remote name or default branch (e.g., `upstream/master`), the target silently creates worktrees from the wrong base.
scenarios: A fork or enterprise repo uses `upstream` as the canonical remote; the target fetches from `origin` and bases the branch on the wrong ref.
Repo migrates default branch to `main` from `master` or vice versa.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `remote ?= origin` and `base ?= main` near the target definition, and use `$(remote)/$(base)` in the recipe, so `make worktree name=foo remote=upstream base=master` works without editing the Makefile.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error handling to the `worktree` recipe: define what happens (abort + cleanup, or clear error message) when `git fetch`, `git worktree add`, `python3 -m venv`, or `make install` fails, and specify whether a partial worktree/branch is removed on failure.
evidence: AC1 only guards the missing `name=` case: 'make worktree with NO name= prints a usage message and exits non-zero'
Plan names no error handling for `git fetch`, `git worktree add`, `python3 -m venv`, or inner `$(MAKE) install` failure modes
ABSENCE: no cleanup/rollback step described anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan specifies only the missing-name validation error path; mid-run failures (git fetch fails/no remote, worktree directory already exists, branch name already taken, python3 unavailable, `make install` fails inside the worktree) are unaddressed — the recipe will silently leave partial state (orphaned worktree directory, partially-created branch, half-provisioned venv) with no cleanup or clear error message.
findings.location: ## What — recipe description; Acceptance Criteria AC1
scenarios: Network unavailable: `git fetch origin` fails mid-run, recipe silently continues or exits without removing any partial state
Branch `<name>` already exists locally: `git worktree add -b <name>` fails, leaving no worktree but confusing output
Directory `../<name>` already exists: worktree add fails with a cryptic git error
`python3` absent or venv creation fails: recipe exits leaving an empty worktree directory and a dangling branch
`make install` fails inside worktree: leaves a broken half-provisioned venv with no hint to the user
findings.suggested_fix: In the recipe, use `set -e`-equivalent shell guards (each line already exits on error in make's default shell, but consider `|| (git worktree remove --force $(dir) 2>/dev/null; git branch -D $(name) 2>/dev/null; exit 1)` after the `git worktree add` call) and add a note in the plan that any step failure triggers cleanup of the partially-created worktree and branch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the false premise that `commit-msg` is auto-inherited: the repo's own CONTRIBUTING.md and SKILL.md confirm a fresh worktree does NOT carry it — add a `curl -Lo .git/hooks/commit-msg … && chmod +x` step to the recipe (matching CONTRIBUTING.md §1b) or at minimum remove the exclusion note and add the install step.
evidence: CONTRIBUTING.md §4e: 'a fresh worktree/clone does not have the hook'
CONTRIBUTING.md troubleshooting: 'The commit-msg hook isn't installed in this checkout — a fresh worktree/clone does not carry it (§4e)'
examples/claude-skills/rebar-implement/SKILL.md line 82: 'a fresh worktree does not carry the hook — install it'
Plan text: 'the target does not re-fetch it — a note in the recipe records why' — an active work exclusion based on the false premise
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The Gerrit commit-msg hook is inherited automatically (worktrees share the common git dir's hooks)' and uses this to exclude a hook-install step from the recipe. Three independent repo sources refute this: CONTRIBUTING.md §4e states 'a fresh worktree/clone does not have the hook'; the rebar-implement SKILL.md Phase 1 step 3 says 'a fresh worktree does not carry the hook — install it'; and CONTRIBUTING.md's troubleshooting section says 'The commit-msg hook isn't installed in this checkout — a fresh worktree/clone does not carry it'. The commit-msg hook is installed per-clone/checkout, not shared from the common git dir. By excluding the install step on a false premise, the target will leave worktrees without a Change-Id hook, and every push to Gerrit will be rejected with 'missing Change-Id in commit message footer'.
findings.location: What section — 'The Gerrit commit-msg hook is inherited automatically (worktrees share the common git dir's hooks), so the target does not re-fetch it — a note in the recipe records why.'
scenarios: A developer or agent uses `make worktree name=my-feature` to bootstrap a worktree, then attempts `git commit` and `git push gerrit HEAD:refs/for/main` — Gerrit rejects the push with 'missing Change-Id in commit message footer' because the commit-msg hook was never installed.

An agent following the SKILL.md workflow relies on `make worktree` as the one-command setup (as advertised), but still needs to manually install the commit-msg hook after — violating the 'one command' premise and the 'stops hand-running the multi-command worktree setup' goal.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the recipe after `git worktree add`: `curl -sLo "$$dir/.git/hooks/commit-msg" https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/tools/hooks/commit-msg && chmod +x "$$dir/.git/hooks/commit-msg"` — matching the pattern already in CONTRIBUTING.md §1b, infra/gerrit/reviewbot-e2e.sh, and the feature-branch e2e harness.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add CLAUDE.md to the edit scope: update the 'Work in a fresh worktree' block (around lines 537–540) to reference `make worktree name=<branch>` as the canonical one-command form, consistent with the docs/local-dev-env.md update.
evidence: CLAUDE.md line 538: 'git fetch origin && git worktree add ../<name> -b <branch> origin/main' — the exact manual sequence the new target automates
Scope section: 'docs/local-dev-env.md: one line pointing at make worktree name=<branch> as the one-command form' — CLAUDE.md not mentioned

CLAUDE.md line 16-17: 'When developing rebar itself … run the repo checkout's build … see docs/local-dev-env.md' — CLAUDE.md delegates env setup to local-dev-env.md, but the worktree block is inline and not delegated
findings.finding: CLAUDE.md contains its own inline worktree setup instructions (the 'Work in a fresh worktree' block at lines 537–540) that prescribe the manual multi-command sequence. The plan enumerates only docs/local-dev-env.md for update, leaving CLAUDE.md — the primary agent-facing guide — inconsistently pointing agents to the manual steps rather than `make worktree name=<branch>`.
scenarios: An agent reads CLAUDE.md first (as intended) and sees the manual worktree sequence in the 'Work in a fresh worktree' block, never discovering `make worktree` — the target exists but agents following the primary guide will not use it.
The two sources diverge over time: local-dev-env.md gets updated with future worktree target changes, CLAUDE.md does not.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a partial-failure cleanup path to the recipe (e.g. a `trap` that runs `git worktree remove --force "$dir" && git branch -D "$name"` on ERR) so a failed run does not leave an orphan worktree+branch that blocks a simple retry.
evidence: Plan describes a linear sequence: git fetch → git worktree add → python3 -m venv → $(MAKE) install, with no trap or rollback.
No mention of error-handling or cleanup of partial state anywhere in the plan.

Cached review finding corroborates: '.rebar/cache/plan-review/11c3-d318-e535-480c/fb512e05541e1d5eb763.json' — 'The Makefile recipe has no rollback/cleanup path for partial failure: if git worktree add succeeds but the subsequent python3 -m venv .venv or $(MAKE) install step fails, the developer is left with an orphan worktree.'
findings.finding: The recipe has no partial-failure cleanup path: if `git worktree add` succeeds but `python3 -m venv` or `$(MAKE) install` subsequently fails, the branch and unpopulated worktree are left as orphans. A re-run with the same `name=` then fails immediately at `git worktree add` ('fatal: branch already exists'), leaving the user stuck with manual `git worktree remove --force ../<name> && git branch -D <name>` cleanup that is not documented in the plan.
findings.location: What section — recipe steps / mechanism correctness
scenarios: Network failure during `$(MAKE) install` (pip index unreachable) — worktree and branch exist, venv is half-populated or absent.
Python version mismatch causes `python3 -m venv` to fail — worktree and branch exist but are empty.
Second `make worktree name=<same>` attempt after any failure above — fails at `git worktree add` with 'branch already exists', requiring manual intervention.
findings.suggested_fix: In the recipe shell, set `set -e` (or use `bash -e`) and add an ERR trap before `git worktree add`: `trap 'git worktree remove --force "$$dir" 2>/dev/null; git branch -D "$$name" 2>/dev/null; echo "worktree: cleaned up after failure"' ERR`. This makes the target idempotent-on-retry by ensuring the partially-created state is removed on any step failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the Makefile recipe must `cd` into the new worktree directory (e.g. `cd <dir> && <venv-activate> && $(MAKE) install`) before invoking `$(MAKE) install`, since a bare `$(MAKE) install` runs in the main checkout, not in `../<name>`.
evidence: Plan: 'with the venv active, $(MAKE) install (editable .[dev] install + make hooks wiring the pre-commit gate)'
Makefile:36: `python -m pip install -e '.[dev]'` — this runs in $PWD, which is the main checkout dir when the recipe starts
The worktree is created at `../<name>` (a sibling directory), so the recipe must explicitly change into that directory before invoking $(MAKE) install
Partially-verified: git worktree add creates the new tree at a sibling path; Make recipes do not auto-cd
findings.finding: The plan says 'with the venv active, $(MAKE) install', but a bare `$(MAKE) install` in a Makefile recipe runs in the current working directory (the main checkout), not in the newly created worktree at `../<name>`. The `install` target would install into the main checkout's environment rather than the new worktree's `.venv`, silently defeating the purpose of the target.
findings.location: What section: 'Provisions the venv via … $(MAKE) install'
scenarios: Developer runs `make worktree name=my-feature`; the recipe creates the worktree and venv at `../my-feature/.venv`, but then `$(MAKE) install` runs in the main checkout directory, editable-installing into the main checkout's active Python rather than the worktree's venv.
The worktree is left with an empty or unpopulated `.venv`, so AC-2's `test -x ../__wt_probe/.venv/bin/rebar` fails.
findings.suggested_fix: In the recipe, use a single compound shell line: `cd $(dir) && python3 -m venv .venv && PATH="$(dir)/.venv/bin:$$PATH" $(MAKE) install`. This ensures both the venv creation and the `make install` run inside the new worktree, and the activated PATH makes `python` and `pip` resolve from the new venv.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the shell mechanism for venv activation inside the Makefile recipe (e.g. a single compound `cd <dir> && PATH=".venv/bin:$$PATH" $(MAKE) install` line, or `.ONESHELL`), since separate recipe lines run in separate subshells and a bare `source .venv/bin/activate` does not persist across lines.
evidence: Plan: 'python3 -m venv .venv (seeds pip so the canonical installer works) then, with the venv active, $(MAKE) install'
Standard Make behavior: each line in a recipe is a separate `/bin/sh -c` invocation; `source .venv/bin/activate` in one line does not persist to the next
Unverified: the plan does not describe a `.ONESHELL`, compound-command, or PATH-prepend mechanism to keep the venv active across recipe lines
findings.finding: The plan says 'with the venv active, $(MAKE) install' but does not address how venv activation is achieved within a Makefile recipe. Each recipe line runs in a separate subshell, so `source .venv/bin/activate` on one line cannot export PATH to a subsequent `$(MAKE) install` line.
findings.location: What section: 'Provisions the venv via … python3 -m venv .venv … then, with the venv active, $(MAKE) install'
scenarios: Author writes a two-line recipe: `source .venv/bin/activate` then `$(MAKE) install`. The second line spawns a fresh shell without the venv active, so `python -m pip install` resolves the system Python, not the worktree venv.
The install silently succeeds but installs into the system Python or a wrong venv.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a single compound shell line with explicit PATH prepend: `cd $(dir) && python3 -m venv .venv && PATH="$$PWD/.venv/bin:$$PATH" $(MAKE) install`, which avoids `source` entirely and works in any POSIX sh.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
